speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Two sisters, Lena Erickson Justice and May Erickson Lamar, recall picking and selling huckleberries, staying at home during the long winters at Hog Meadows and the Wells family at Deary. They both flunkied at logging camps and they described their duties and some of their experiences. Lena's husband, Albert, was a cook and she remembers his cooking and wanted to quit.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: During World War I we did not have huckleberries.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Where would you sell them?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: There would be other people that would come in from outside to pick huckleberries also. They would see us with our berries and they liked them up too well so they would buy what we had picked that day. We didn't even get home with them. So we'd go home with empty pails which was fine by us. Couple of doors in our pocket. That meant a lot. There again it helped provide the school clothes and books and things that we needed for school in those days.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Wasn't it a dollar a gallon we got from them?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: It seemed like one time we got three dollars a gallon during the first World War. Two dollars a gallon during World War I.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Two dollars a gallon?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: In a way we thought it was a lot.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: It was quite a bit in those days. But ordinarily they would sell for a dollar a gallon so everything else went up in price. And we're still selling berries.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: We're getting five dollars a gallon now and we're getting rich. Doesn't buy what it bought back then though. No that's right. You can buy a bunch now. A tank of gas. Don't even do that. The little car even takes more than that. The big one takes about ten.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: With the two of you, did you do a lot of things together?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Quite a bit. I was about eleven then. In 1918 I was eleven. I was ten in 1917. In 1918 we were picking a lot of berries.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: I was eleven years old. Pretty good size girl. I could pick my share I guess. You sure could. She still can. She's a better picker than I am and I've been picking for sixty -five years. May has been picking for sixty years. About sixty I guess.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: I sold my first half of berries when I was nine I think. Only three kids were out picking. No the four of us. The two boys. Molly and I. I called Molly. That's my name. So at the end of the time we had our pails. I had a half a gallon large pail. The other kids had their gallon buckets. So this fella said well I'll just buy your half of berries from you. So he gave the other kids each a dollar. So here he gave me a dollar for my half a gallon. And Alfred, you know my brother that was here, he said oh my goodness that's too much. He said she only has a half a gallon. Well he said she worked just as hard as the other kids. I bet you did. So I had my dollar for my half a gallon. I was pretty proud about that. Big silver dollar in those days. Not to pay for money. But that was a big help in bringing in a little extra money when money was so hard to get hold of in those days.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you go out often to pick berries?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yes we'd pick all during the season when the huckleberries would rise from about in oh after the 4th usually after the 4th of July.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: And we could pick then up until oh about the first of August. And people used to come out to the house, even people, our local people you know from Bovill, they'd come out there we'd know they knew we had huckleberries so they'd come out there and buy them from us. Or sometimes we'd take them in, sometimes we'd take them into our grocery and trade them for groceries. So it worked in a lot of ways that way to our advantage. Because that gives us a chance to pick up a little extra money.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you go out every day?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Usually I would. As a rule we'd go out every day. Yes we'd make a regular job of it. Except when well during haying we might have to lay off and help you know shock hay that we did out in the fields and the hay would be mowed down we'd have to put it in shock so they could go along and pick it up on a wagon on a hay rack and put it in the barn. So we've had to help on that in between times but as a rule we'd stay with our berry picking pretty regular. Although not every year we'd have berries that's another thing because usually every other year the berry crop would be good.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You figured every other?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Usually every other year that they would be good. But there would always be some somewhere but nearby but they don't produce equally good each year. That's the only thing about it. But we enjoyed it. It was kind of hard work but we always liked it too being out in the woods and making a little extra money.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: I've heard the Indians used to you know they would shake the berry bush.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yes they used to cut the bush isn't that I can't understand because they would you would think they'd want to preserve things like that more and that would I know it would take some time for that bush to grow back but I've heard of it I never did see it but I have seen huge piles of broken huckleberry bushes and they'd beat them off onto a blanket and I guess the blanket might be worn or something like that and then they could shake it a little bit like that and the leaves and all the debris would stick to the blanket and the berries would roll off and the berries they would also sell their berries I think to a large extent. I've heard of them doing that but I never did see them do it but I think my sister has when she comes back down I think she can tell us somewhat about that too. I never liked that idea I'd rather just go and pick them I wouldn't see them it would be much fun you know working on a bunch of broken bushes.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were you able to go to school?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Not very much no in fact I didn't start school until I was almost 11 but I could read and write fairly well before I attended school. Learned that at home well actually we had a first grade reader I guess at home and I got a little start I think my brother got me started on that her mother did one or the other but I just naturally liked to read so I just picked it up so and I didn't get to school then until I was almost 11 but I was promoted to the third grade at the end of that school term and that being that I could read I guess and enjoyed it I was always promoted so I got through grade school in a hurry that way I always made two grades a year or more. I can't imagine a life without reading that was one of the things I still enjoy doing.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What were you able to what was what kind of books were around then that you could read?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well I wonder if I can remember let's see I wish I had kept them but I didn't but they were well Alice in Wonderland and the Fisherman's Wife and oh goodness can I even begin to remember back all the things we had but they were those stories after that fashion and Three Little Pigs I guess that would be in the oh second grade reader I guess I don't know if they would be called McGuffey's reader or not I suppose they would I think maybe it would be something in that order but that would be the type of stories we had to read. Black Beauty and oh my goodness and then as we got a little bit further along in the grades there would be all this a lot of poems by Longfellow and all notes you know we'd have to memorize things like that that'd be up in the around the sixth and seventh grade we'd have that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were they still having literarys when they were young?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yes I think they did but I used to have that in Helmer they have to call literary meetings but I never attended I was too small then much too young but I now my brother Alfred would know about that he's attended some but I never did go to one of those but then too that was part of ways away that would be usually held at night you know and be about four miles away and usually in the winter when there'd be four or five foot of snow on the ground so as due to that a person was more or less isolated in the winter it would be pretty hard to get out very much.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you would you just stay at home?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yes that's about all we did was just stay at home and the boys kept busy of course chopping wood and everything of course they used to sell wood you know chop down trees and sell wood by the cord in Bowell and all that out and then to go out for the mail once a week go to for a while it was at Helmer then we moved it to Bowell where her mail came to so they would have to go there and get the mail and we had weekly newspapers in those days mother would get her Norwegian paper that came from Decorah Iowa and that was the weekly paper and the spokesman review that was the weekly paper finally I think they got up to where it was twice a weekend we just mailed out to subscribers and we also had a magazine was called the Idaho farmer that was published here in Spokane I think near as I remember and that would be seemed like that came once a month now I'd have to ask my sister about and that would have stories in it continued stories you know so we all look forward to that because it'd be something interesting to read I'd have to ask my sister about it but no usually we'd read it to ourselves although mother spoke both English and Norwegian but Norwegian she spoke fluently of course and English not too clear but mother would read from her paper there was also stories in her Norwegian paper that she would read out loud to us in Norwegian which we understood very well so that was another diversion was your father Norwegian or yes he was Norwegian and did you learn Norwegian at home was that well it was a strange setup we had on that I don't know I'll never explain be able to explain how but mother always talked Norwegian to us at home so I just grew up with that but we answered on English now I don't know why it seems strange I should be able to speak it fluently but I don't I can get by make myself understood but we could mom and all say something Norwegian to us and we'd always answer in English invariably but dad spoke Norwegian excellently of course as well as English he spoke that very well so with mother though it was a little bit more hauling with her she'd get some of but she could read and write both dad I think only read Norwegian I don't think he read English too well he did Norwegian but I can't understand how that happened how did you pick up your English from other kids in the neighborhood or from your father no no we just I don't know mostly from dad I don't really know I can't because far back as I can remember she always talked Norwegian to us and we'd answer in English so but there we didn't have any close neighbors you know and had children our own age I guess my sister did more but then when I came along more than six years younger than my sister well there weren't any kids around my age then all the other people had moved away and any youngsters I want to ask you your sister mentioned that you'd seen the Indians pick berries in the woods oh yeah you've seen them and break bushes break bushes I think in the squaw what they call them the squaws they sit down and pick the berries and the men or was it the vice versa I remember now they probably go out and break the bushes and the squaws used to have to do all the work anyway so I imagine they had to break the bushes and bring it to the yeah it could be I never did see them do it but you know I've seen the bushes but I think you can see how high this room you saw them do it then oh yeah I never did because I was a little bit poor in my time because the Indians didn't come around well not quite as much and I began to diminish somewhat didn't it as time went on yeah I remember them coming there and getting water out of this well I was telling you about that was such good spring water coming up on the horse and they'd fill their little sacks and they'd take them out of the sacks and they'd stack the water breaking water yeah and dad had to get after them because they were digging camas and the medellin on made it so rough when they mowed the hay and I guess one of them was threatening wasn't he or something more dead eating it for you yeah I think that's what dad said because he chased them out of the meadow that's what they made their bread

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: walked over there and my dad came after me and went just eating dinner I can remember how I must have been awful naughty I was kicking the table he was grabbing me and I was kicking away and I didn't want to go home I wanted to eat but I got it afterwards when I got home I got a spanking I can remember that that's before me I guess I must have been about four years old but that's about all I remember of any unusual things

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there people in Bogle that did you go were you able to ever have ice cream or candy in Bogle?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: no well my dad whenever he'd go to town they'd always buy a nickels word of candy and you got a pretty big sack of candy for a nickel you know he'd come home with candy or maybe some oranges or bananas or something like that it all depends on how they could afford it you know if they had any money left and then you used to go to Deary most of the trading and that was about eight miles and of course we'd whenever he paid up his bill why they'd give him quite a bit of a home sack of candy maybe about a pound or a pound and a half something like that of course we was all hungry for sweets so that didn't last too long

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: and when my brother went to school down at Helmer we used to have a Christmas program down there and of course I was too small to go at that time but anyway we'd always I think my dad donated oh maybe two bits or something like that for a sack of candy and orange and apples and stuff like that we'd get and we'd come home with that and pour peanuts and that would be our treat for Christmas and maybe we'd have a little tree or something and we'd have to make our own decoration catalog you know and be colored pieces and we'd paste them together and string them around on the tree and that was the first time you ever did that when you was in well not with the catalog no well I had the pretty pictures on us either and popcorn sometimes we'd string popcorn and stuff like that for Christmas make it look a little Christmas but as far as eats go we didn't have turkey or anything like that did your parents keep any of the Norwegian costumes?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: a mother did have but we moved so much it seemed like we got the only thing I got which I think would be nice if you wanted for the museum is her wedding veil it's kind of tattered and I have her have their wedding picture I was asking whether they kept customs Norwegian customs you know any of the traditions from Norway oh her mother wasn't born in Norway she was born in the United States she was born in Illinois but her mother and dad and her sisters her oldest sister the mother was the baby of the family and they were from Norway and I think if I remember right that she said something about I guess a brother next to her must have been I had died when they were on the ship and he was buried in the sea because he couldn't take the body on you know but they went to Illinois, Bournemouth County Illinois I guess is where he was then they moved to Minnesota is where she was about what did she say she was baby I guess maybe about a year old or something like that would your parents speak Norwegian to each other? oh yes my mother spoke Norwegian all the time but she could speak English very good and she wrote Norwegian and English both read the papers and all that and the Bible of course that was her main thing we'd have to be awfully quiet when she read the Bible to us we had to pay attention would that be Norwegian she'd read that?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: yeah she'd read Norwegian yeah we could speak a little Norwegian May and I went to Norway five years ago I had a wonderful time there trying to find some of our relatives there but we didn't dad he always talked English to us girls and kids brother but mother had always talked Norwegian because she wanted us to know the Norwegian language but dad and then when they talked well then they used the Norwegian language together but we could understand I was talking to my brother here not too long ago and I'd say something in Norwegian and he's forgotten he hasn't been around for well since mother died and that's been several years ago, 45, 46 I guess something like that but when you're around it all comes back to you so you can you know

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: yeah it's quite a thing I wish I'd have learned to read it and write it

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: of course there wasn't any Norwegian schools around there now like here in Spokane their sons are Norway they have schools and anybody around and she was a midwife I think but they came from the south boy was she ever a good cook, wonderful cook at her place I remember the first time I ate there you know how you kind of feel like you look clean but she was clean as could be very much so

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I've heard a fair bit about them but were they, do you think they were well accepted by you? in Deary?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yes they were, it was funny one time the old Joe Wells said well you know he says I'm the only white man here he says they all, the rest of them are Swedes, kind of cute and he was black as ace of spades, he was black she was too but they were yes they were pretty nice people except for Domeniwe and later on the girls their daughter got married and they had a couple of children I think and the daughter was a very smart girl she was valedictorian but you know they didn't something about it because she was black they didn't want to give it to her but I guess the rest of them kicked about it so she got it and I thought that was kind of mean

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was she much older than you? she would have been, wouldn't she?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh yeah...

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: The granddaughter wouldn't have been older than I am because I can remember when she was born because I went up to see her and wanted to see what she looks like, what the baby looked like kind of cute, holding her over the back oh my goodness, that's on the east west side of the money that was the main thing, get out and earn some money they were doing pretty hard times too, what year was it we went down there? let's see we worked, let me think, 1923 we went down to Princeton to work 1923, yeah that's right because you got married the next year in 1924 right?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: That's right well you worked up by Chez Meadows for a short time yeah that's where we first had 1922 then, summer of 1922 we were going up to get some school money

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah that's 1922 and then we went down to camp one

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: And then from there to camp seven back to Chez Meadows again

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: How long did you work there? I can't remember camp seven?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah see I started at Princeton and in December we were transferred to camp seven and I worked there until the following August I think and I quit and went back to finish grade school oh that's right went back to school, finished that

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: And then I went back to work I guess the next year I went back to work at Logie camp I worked on and off for about, let's see about seven and a half or eight years for the Potlach Lumber Company you worked for Billy Must didn't you? I worked at headquarters that was in 1928 when I was there for a while were they just starting up that logging that area when you went out there? no, camps around Elk River had been running for quite a number of years but they had built better camps they'd enlarge them a lot and they called it camp C headquarters where it was tied up where the railroad or the logging engines would come in for the night they kept them there, that was the base for them and then they'd go out to the other two or three camps and pick up logs during the day but the trains were always tied up there at camp C at night that was near Elk River? yes, well how about 14 miles from Elk River wasn't it? way to Crow Fies I guess about 14, 15 were you out there then?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: No, let's see we were down at camp five I guess on Hog Meadows yes I think you were oh shoot then you were transferred up to camp D to Elk River yeah I stayed down at the Hog Meadows all alone, or Betty and I I'd bar myself into the shack and put knives up so everybody could get in I was kind of worried about being by ourself in the morning I'd go down and get breakfast in the logging camp, they had the whole camp there then I'd go clear down and start the pump at the pump house start that at night, see if it'd run alright and then build a fire down there, kept the fire going so it wouldn't freeze up it was in the winter time and then Betty and I go back to her shack and bar ourselves in I don't know what good it done if anybody wanted to get in

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Then from there they got a place for us up at camp D where Albert was cooking they took him up there, so he was there for her then you came up there later yes I came up to work later then yeah, first I went to camp that's where she met her, her first husband camp D

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: I was there until 1930 then, about two years up there yeah

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: when you worked together were your jobs any different than each others? no, when we worked together we'd done the same thing I mean wait table and that's down in Princeton and now we're at camp 7 also now they serve family style you know in logging camps our job was to keep the dishes filled, vegetables and meats what would you have to get up at 4 .30 for? what happened at 4 .30 in the morning? well you had a lot to do, you had to get your tables of course we had the silverware and the plates on and the cups and all that we'd put those on the night before but there would be butter to put on the tables and milk and fruit and things for their breakfast, syrup and all their cookies and donuts and bread yeah, and bread, we also had to have bread on the table and we'd always slice the bread, be somebody who would want it because in addition to that then they'd have pancakes every morning and toast and toast hash brown potatoes, bacon, eggs would they have eggs every morning?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Every morning...

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: scrambled, boiled, fried they'd have hot cereal and we'd have to put milk on for their cereal so we'd have to dilute canned milk the way we did it put that on as well as a can of milk for their coffee, for their coffee cream and you'd dilute a certain amount of that milk for their cereal oh and then we always had dry cereal and corn flakes and shredded wheat maybe and all bran I think, we didn't have as many varieties then as we do now and that always had to be put on the table so there would be a lot to put up and then we'd have to fix the table so they could pack their own lunches get out the bread and the butter for that yeah, took quite a while to get those ready there'd be a lot of things to get ready and then breakfast was always served at six so then the cooks would have the hot meal, the hard part of the meal ready like the pancakes and bacon and eggs we'd set that on the table it was like the thundering heard as soon as the doors opened and we unlocked the door here they come clumpity clump

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: I was blowing it real hard, it would carry too that sound and the lumberjack come in and they'd eat and they'd eat in silence in order to get through in a hurry because there wasn't any sitting and smoking at the tables lingering over their coffee as soon as they'd be through they'd leave and head out for the woods where they'd work

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Did they end up switching to store bought bread? or did they just make their own? not when we were in there or when we were working they might have did later years now, I don't know I was in the Albert was a godsend when they got sliced bacon oh yes, they had to slice their own always yeah, gee that sliced up three or four slabs of bacon I think quite a while it takes time and they fry them in the great big pans that long and about that wide I guess and they lay it in layers should probably pop down here for the meat? up there yeah, heat the water there for your coffee you mean they'd bake it in the oven? yeah, they bake it in the oven I had bacon, that's an excellent way to cook bacon they just put it in the layers kind of you know kind of thick and then you take some fry it out same with making scrambled eggs put that in the oven too big pans of that and you just keep working them they also used to fry their potatoes you know when you slice them instead of the hash browns you know what I mean like cutting up your raw potatoes and putting them in the oven remember Albert doing that? they have a great big baking pan quite deep, about so deep I guess big square pan, you'd have that full of potatoes you'd pull it out every once in a while and turn them over so they cook equally remember how tender? would he have a lot of oil in it? quite a bit, they used quite a lot of bacon drippings I think to season it out, they made them taste good how would they cook that many pancakes? and have them warm for the men? well they had big grills big as a stable isn't it May?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh yes, I would say it was as long as that and you get that full of...

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: You ought to see how he had that batter plunk plunk plunk plunk plunk plunk and then it'd become a flippin '... flip flip flip and then he'd keep flipping

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Then we'd go with the pans, hold them there and he'd slap them on our pan and away we'd go and the lumberjacks take the whole...

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yes, I would take... you'd put on about four or five on each plate that we'd bring back for refills that way we always kept them coming hot they were sourdough pancakes they were good

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: He used the sourdough starter?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: oh yeah, that's why we... I don't know how he started it, with yeast I think when he'd go into a camp yes, he'd do it, save out a certain amount each time and then mix up, and they always have some for the next morning some cook says when the... you know, the dough keeps getting... the hole is getting smaller in the pan the old pot that keeps the sourdough in keeps getting smaller and smaller then you have to cut out that... take it off and throw it away yeah, I've seen that you know, you keep stirring putting the dough back in the same crock or whatever you use you stir your flour in there and your eggs and then you pour it out into a big pan they were good hotcakes, so... I've tried to make some, but I'm not so good at...

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: It's experience, I guess. He ever had biscuits in the morning?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: I don't think he ever did he had those at night for dinner sometimes you'd always have hot rolls for dinner because he baked bread so nearly always had buns seemed like the biscuits them days were so good I can't make biscuits like that well, anything Albert makes is good I don't care, he's the best cook I don't think he can make as good from the flour we get now he had the hard wheat, the soft wheat it's so much different flour has something to do with it I even kept...

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: I used to make pretty good bread but I can't make bread more too many chemicals used now maybe in it they used to have separate wheat for the hard and soft and used it for different things hard wheat for bread and soft wheat for biscuits and cakes two different bins did you use any other kind of flour at all? cornmeal? oh yeah, I had cornmeal oh yes, he'd make cornbread yeah, cornbread yeah, a certain time whole wheat made whole wheat bread too yes, I think he did yeah, he could have that so they could have a choice we used to have two different kinds of bread on the plate he made rye bread yeah, rye there were three kinds of cakes and usually two kinds of pies did you make any of the food itself?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: or did you...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well, not so much we were usually busy with our dining room work but we'd ice cake sometime if they got fried donuts we'd help them out if they got a little bit pushed, you know, for help if they had some days there'd be more things to get out maybe than other times I remember doing that, frying donuts turning them we'd be bad at grease and we'd use lard they used lard instead of shortening, didn't they? for donuts?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, I think so and he used lard with a pie crust and he used some of the drippings too, I think from like your...

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, from beef, you know a lot of fat when he cut up the beef he used that towel or whatever it is mix it mix it with the lard that's what I left for delicious would he get a whole side of beef and cut it up himself? oh yeah he always cut his own meat that was an awful big, heavy job because he used to handle those hind quarters I don't know he sure did, I don't know he'd get it on his back and take it over on a great big block that they'd have you know, what they had in the meat house that was always a little place right next to the cook shack and he'd be all screened in keep it cool then he'd have to cut off the steaks and stews and roasts did all of that himself from the meat so that took a lot of time and then they'd make their own hamburger remember, and then grind it with a hand grinder that was a rough job too right bunch of lumberjacks he was telling them about, I didn't know that when he first started to learn to cook this Gertrch, what was his name? wasn't him either some old cook, he said he'd tell him what to do and he'd sit in a chair oh?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: he'd sit in a chair and tell Albert what to do so much of this and so much of that to make bread, you know oh yeah and that's when he first started, he said he was talking about it here the other morning

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Gertrchow, Gertrchow wasn't that the name of that one cook that cooked? yeah he never liked a large crew, he was the section, on the section gang oh, he worked on that I remember he was a dandy cook though, I guess they said, or Albert said he was wonderful cook and so clean oh, he was neat I think I went into his bunk or his cook house one time and you could eat off of that floor always kept things scrubbed, just like a pen but he never had a big crew, he had about I don't know how many they had on the section, about 40 yeah, that'd be about, I think, between 25 and 40 not too large anytime you push a big crew on him, he'd pull out that was quite a headache, they had to do a lot we used to have to wash dishes too I don't know, when we were in the smaller you know, camp 7, we'd wash dishes and did our dining room work peel the vegetables, take the floors clean you remember when we had extra men come in on us and we crowded yeah, we had to do a lot of days of being stormy we crowded them in on this long table and they were growling, the other guys moved out they had long benches, you know and they were growling because they had to move down we didn't have room for them to sit in the other place sure it didn't so we had to send them in and they wanted to eat and the center table full we had two long tables and a center table we had to train crews there and this was a stormy day though when it was too stormy for them to go out and do their work, they'd have to stay in it was snowing so bad and we'd have them all for lunch usually lunch would be a little bit light because they'd be out in the woods with their lunches oh my, you're watching chickens on the roost picking the other ones head would that happen often that they couldn't go out to work? oh, once in a while they wouldn't get any pay then, would they? no, no that'd be it they'd have to pay their board then I don't remember what it I just wonder what they did come out with a day more than room what did you get when you started? when did we get mail? we got 60 a month more than room 7 days a week day no time off you didn't have any time off? no, 7 day a week we put in not even an afternoon oh yeah, we'd have maybe an hour or so in the afternoon we worked all morning though, usually yeah, usually we were busy during the morning not very often we'd get in time we'd have a peeled sack of onions you know how that, we had a crying jag oh yeah, and carrots did you have any ways to get around that that ever worked? you know, to stop you from crying?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: oh, we'd just cry we'd just cry yeah, we didn't have any methods and then we'd have french fries so we also had to do those with a paring knife peel our potatoes and then cut it into french fries then we'd have parsnips and carrots and rutabagas doing different vegetables, fresh vegetables that would be used they didn't get canned carrots then I think I ever did see any no you'd get canned string beans string beans and peas, I guess tomatoes we'd want fried onions, maybe fried onions and liver or something like that, so that meant slicing the onions the more we cried

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: we always had to pay a peel of a sack of potatoes, didn't we? or a sack and a half didn't have a peeler either oh no, we did that on hand peeling I mean like you and one of the peelers we did a paring knife a paring knife real sharp and we'd have to wet our fingers we always wrapped mine to keep from getting caught it was a lot of fun, though I enjoyed that work very much in the logging camp I liked the timber anyway what was fun about it?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: well, it was hard work but I liked it out in the woods, fresh air and in the afternoon if we wanted to go for a walk we'd go for a walk or go down for a bump and take a little nap

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: it was sometimes we'd walk in the woods where they were logging and you know, just walk the railroad track, it would be quite safe go up there and pull across, got saws sometimes with some of the fellas we knew, you know falling timber and they thought that was pretty good

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: scalers

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Used to call me tiny big Swede you remember him? Paul Freese?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: I was kind of hefty, I guess they used to call you what? tiny, tiny big Swede, he says I can't even think of that well, you ever come to call me that? I don't know, you thought you was kind of a husky gal I guess you were then, but you weren't later, you got rid of some of the weight I weighed around 150, I guess 55, I don't know somewhere around that what did you weigh one time? wasn't you about 125 at one time? on that company scale, I was about that of course that was in fully clothed, you know with clothes we wore then we were quite, we had to dress quite warm it was so cold would you have to reheat the cook houses every morning? or would they be warm? the cook houses? oh, some mornings it wouldn't be too warm when you go in there because the fire hadn't been, especially in the dining room the fire wouldn't be kept going all night but with all we called the bull cook he'd kept the fires going he'd be in quite early and get that big stove going always had a oh, it was kind of a long stove not exactly pot well, it would be a pretty long stove, I don't know it would be a lot from here against that wall, wouldn't it? that big one we had in one of the camps?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: took up quite a bit of room fill that full of wood and get it going so it would heat up the dining room pretty well and of course the fellas sitting near the stove would get warmer than those way down to further into the dining room gosh, this one up at camp D when we worked up there that was the coldest spot ooh, that was cold yeah, many mornings I had to wear my galoshes to wait tables in my feet would get so cold because they were on cars, you know they were movable cars they were on wheels and they'd be up off the ground quite a ways so nothing underneath so that cold would come off and they'd be up around all around under the floors boy, it would be cool you'd start to warm up much around noon my deck was 35 and 40 below up there oh yeah, it must have been snow was deep snow some days was snow two or three days at a stretch just keep piling up

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, we moved to Bovia after she had to go she went to school up here her grandmother for oh, I don't know, maybe about a month it didn't prove out so I moved to Bovill and got a house there so she started school

words: She graduated from Bovill and then went to University of Idaho

words: Yeah, I guess so she graduated from U of I in 47 she became a 47 oh, 42 is when we I guess moved up here then she taught one year in Lewiston and then she got married

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Married 25 years last year 26 years she had been married now this year it all seemed quite plausible when you lived in Bovill would your husband come in on weekends at all?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: About every other week

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: every other week? you mean they would work out a week?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, like Ralph Hanson he would take over one weekend and then it would switch so that is how he got in of course when he got to be alone in the camp then he had to stay there all the time and Betty and I would take a trip to camp maybe stay over or something like that in the winter though I bet you couldn't could you and you couldn't get back there and drive well I did drive to I can't remember what camp camp 6 I think it was and another camp that was quite a ways out and I can remember the snow was so high you wouldn't get off the road anyway squeaky snow you know the clouds we would go out there maybe and just go out for the day like on Sunday or something like that

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: I driven to Elk River well you know I rode from Bovill to camp D and to C camps yeah camp C then we would have to walk from camp C up to old camp or new camp D.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: that was about a mile and a half I guess something like that walked a little which I that was kind of hard job because one year I was sick and I drove the car for another lady and god I could hardly make it up there I don't know I had to run a pneumonia or something darn thing that was terrible it was so weak I bet you had virus pneumonia I don't know I couldn't even stand the smell of the food I had to get out of the kitchen oh my goodness I just couldn't stand it so I went over to Albert's bunkhouse and laid down until we got ready to go home then I decided I better go to the doctor my lungs were all filled up with mucus you just Dr. Wilson he's in the rest home now you know I'm a good doctor poor man I went to see him but I don't think he remembered me but

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: He sure was a was a nice guy when mother was sick he'd come and see her you know come to the bowl and remember me I sure do he came up, gave her an examination and check over very very nice hard to beat

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well we're sure you're giving the little dog a good I think he's going to have the best one my problem is that we talked so much last time and I asked you most yeah Alfred I think done alright though I think his brother I think our brother was done real good there are some real good stories

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How long did that when you lived in Bovill was that, did you encourage your husband to quit?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: No he just got tired of it they were writing him too much made him oh it just seemed like he was pushing the work on him too much he was up at Clarkia up there that's when he quit up there oh that's when he came to Spokane and they boarded with you guys up here you know yeah they had gee I don't know how many he had 300 and some men and they were just pushing the work on him he said we'll give them the help so he got mad one day and told him he's quit so he came home was home a while and then he thought he'd come up here and he worked for the aluminum wasn't it aluminum seemed like he worked out of Niagara for a while he was the war was starting then you know yeah got in on that uh huh he cooked for the CC boys that was and then the W was it WPA yeah WPA but he he worked for those CC boys what did he want he had a large crew of those cooked for them the boys from the east and all around were from the big cities they had them out there working and well I was going to tell you about when he got sick and I came up after him and I brought him home and got over it first thing he did he got a call from the pot lets come back to cooking again remember he went up to Elk River oh yes and he cooked and he came up out of Elk River there so I took him up there and he decided well he'd go back to work with a small crew so he went up there and worked for the potlatch again for old Axel Anderson and he worked there and then we heard that Mrs. Watts won the cellar restaurant and Mr.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Watts got killed on an automobile accident so we he came in and we said we saw about that so we bought the restaurant in Bovill and run that for about a year when we sold out it was too rough too many hours, that was awful that was a pretty hard goal I guess of course if we'd have had the tavern that's which I wanted to buy is a tavern in connection with the restaurant I knew I could handle the tavern if he'd have handled the restaurant but he didn't want anything to do with the drugs so I thought well I could handle them things I think throw them out on the air or something were the lumberjacks often drunk in the camps? no they'd go out on the bender and then come back what'd they say?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: They didn't allow them at drinking and camp no, of course our boss up there made milk oh yes I used to cut his hair poor guy, he was a nice guy though but he'd like his nips I guess and he had a little too much one time

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: He wanted a haircut I'd done barbering in his camp he was sitting and he was getting down further and I'd have to pull him up he was working down

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: I still do a little barbering now for the people that can't get out I guess Mae barbers her husband's hair he won't go to a barber shop I used to cut my son's hair until he went east to college he never went to a barber shop Albert didn't go to a barber shop until I was working waitress worked down here what I did was on Suvi that wind blowing you know what they used to wear?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: It's just perfect I don't know how I did it now you would know what a wind blowing was like but it's shorter up here and it kinda fluffed around

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: She wanted the wind blowing haircut and I said well I'll try it it just came out perfect of course she had a lot of hair to work with you like you have you got a lot of hair her hair thick though Mae beautiful hair

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well you they put the waves in by just like Marcel we didn't do pin curls them days we used a comb pull your hair this way and then put it down here and bring it back this way and it'd be regular waves

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: And then I'd cut their hair shape it I'd get eggs for it Gilfoy was one of them, his wife there were his kids if you know his boy he used to come over there he got all tired you know that youngest one?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh I don't know about that would a lot of the champs just let their hair grow long? no they'd go out and get a haircut it could get a little long but sometimes they'd go out I didn't have all the long I just had the boss and Albert and maybe the kitchen crew and then I had what's Orville? Orville Myers oh yeah I used to cut his hair I kinda liked my haircut I guess but there wasn't much money in it I couldn't charge because I didn't have a license so they'd give me donations then when we moved to Bovill during the depression I had quite a few up there I even fixed the women's hair giving them finger waves them knees you know what a finger wave is?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Even Tarbox remembers that I saw Walt Tarbox we had a bunch of railroad guys come in this was in the evening and there was a lady here I wouldn't call her a lady I'm bitty in a way I don't care if you want to hear it or not anyway she brought her dog in there and I had to sign up there's no dogs allowed in the restaurant she brought her dog in there so she took some stuff and fit him on the floor and you know grease and stuff like that it's just was too much scrubbing that big floor have you ever been in that restaurant?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well it's about the same way it was when we were there so I said to her I said I had the tables all set up and a bunch of men was on that side and here she was I said to her I says I you know there's no dogs allowed in this restaurant I said there's a sign out there.
